caa_analysis.message: CAA configured - only Let's Encrypt, DigiCert, Amazon, Sectigo can issue certificates
caa_analysis.mpic_note: Since September 2025, all public CAs must verify domain control from multiple geographic locations (Multi-Perspective Issuance Corroboration, CA/B Forum Ballot SC-067). CAA records are now checked from multiple network perspectives before certificate issuance.

dimensions.details: All record data is within its TTL validity window
All resolver TTLs are within authoritative limits (RFC 8767 compliant)
9 of 15 expected record types are missing TTL data
All resolvers return consistent data — high source credibility
Multiple TTLs significantly deviate from standards — review DNS configuration

findings.recommendation: NS TTL is below typical — observed 12092s, typical value is 1 day (86400s). Short TTLs increase DNS query volume but enable faster propagation. If you are preparing for a migration or need rapid failover, this may be intentional (RFC 1035 §3.2.1). For steady-state production, consider 86400 seconds per NIST SP 800-53 SI-7 relevance guidance. Use the TTL Tuner for profile-specific recommendations.

CAA TTL is below typical — observed 5 minutes (300s), typical value is 1 hour (3600s). Short TTLs increase DNS query volume but enable faster propagation. If you are preparing for a migration or need rapid failover, this may be intentional (RFC 1035 §3.2.1). For steady-state production, consider 3600 seconds per NIST SP 800-53 SI-7 relevance guidance. Use the TTL Tuner for profile-specific recommendations.

SOA TTL is below typical — observed 15 minutes (900s), typical value is 1 hour (3600s). Short TTLs increase DNS query volume but enable faster propagation. If you are preparing for a migration or need rapid failover, this may be intentional (RFC 1035 §3.2.1). For steady-state production, consider 3600 seconds per NIST SP 800-53 SI-7 relevance guidance. Use the TTL Tuner for profile-specific recommendations.

dmarc_analysis.message: DMARC in monitoring mode (p=none) - spoofed mail still delivered, no enforcement

ruf_note.detail: The absence of ruf= is not a gap. RFC 7489 §7.3 warns that forensic reports can expose PII (full message headers or bodies). Google, Microsoft, and Yahoo do not honour ruf= requests regardless. The DMARCbis draft (draft-ietf-dmarc-dmarcbis) has formally removed ruf= from the specification, confirming its deprecation. Omitting ruf= is the recommended modern practice.
ruf_note.summary: No forensic reporting (ruf) tag — this is correct.

entries.propagation_note: A records typically propagate within 5 minutes. Some resolvers may cache up to the TTL value.
AAAA records follow the same propagation pattern as A records.
MX record changes may take up to 1 hour to propagate. Mail delivery may be affected during transition.
TXT records (including SPF) typically propagate within 1 hour. Verify with multiple resolvers.
Nameserver changes can take 24–48 hours for full global propagation due to parent zone TTLs.
CNAME changes propagate quickly but downstream records inherit the CNAME TTL.
CAA record changes take effect within TTL. Certificate authorities check at issuance time.
SOA changes propagate to secondaries based on the Refresh interval in the SOA record.
SPF record changes propagate within the TXT record TTL. Test with dig before relying on scan results.
DMARC policy changes at _dmarc subdomain propagate within TTL. Reporting changes take 24–48h to reflect in aggregate reports.
DKIM selector records propagate within TTL. New selectors are available immediately once published; key rotation requires overlap period.
MTA-STS policy changes require updating both the DNS TXT record AND the policy file at /.well-known/mta-sts.txt. The max_age directive in the policy controls how long senders cache it.
TLS-RPT changes propagate within TTL. Report delivery changes take effect in the next reporting period (typically 24 hours).
BIMI record changes propagate within TTL. VMC certificate validation by mail providers may take additional time.
TLSA/DANE records must be published BEFORE rotating TLS certificates. Premature certificate rotation breaks DANE validation.
DNSSEC signing changes (DS record updates at registrar) can take 24–48 hours. Key rollovers require careful timing per RFC 7583.
DANE/TLSA record updates follow the TLSA TTL. Coordinate with TLS certificate lifecycle.

freshness_matrix.guidance: Re-scan times are based on observed TTL values from authoritative and resolver responses. After making DNS changes, wait at least the recommended interval before re-scanning for updated results.

all_fixes.title: DANE Requires DNSSEC

all_fixes.dns_purpose: A quarantine or reject policy instructs receivers to take action on failing mail.
all_fixes.fix: Your DMARC policy is monitor-only (p=none). Upgrade to p=quarantine or p=reject after reviewing reports to actively prevent spoofing.
all_fixes.rfc_title: Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance (DMARC)
all_fixes.title: Upgrade DMARC from p=none

all_fixes.fix: DNSSEC is not enabled for this domain. DNSSEC provides cryptographic authentication of DNS responses, preventing cache poisoning and DNS spoofing attacks.
all_fixes.rfc_title: Protocol Modifications for the DNS Security Extensions
all_fixes.title: Enable DNSSEC

all_fixes.dns_purpose: TLS-RPT sends you reports about TLS connection failures to your mail servers.
all_fixes.fix: Your domain has DNSSEC + DANE — the strongest email transport security available. TLS-RPT (TLS Reporting) sends you reports about TLS connection failures when other servers try to deliver mail to your domain.
all_fixes.title: Add TLS-RPT Reporting

all_fixes.dns_purpose: MTA-STS tells sending servers to require TLS when delivering mail to your domain.
all_fixes.fix: MTA-STS enforces TLS encryption for inbound mail delivery, preventing downgrade attacks on your mail transport.
all_fixes.title: Deploy MTA-STS
